name: Chicken Stock (Caldo de pollo)
subtitle: The slow-simmered soul of Spanish kitchens

enhancement.cookbook_narrative: A great stock is not a recipe so much as a philosophy — the patient extraction of everything a roast chicken leaves behind. Claudia Roden's caldo de pollo asks nothing more than cold water, a carcass, and time; yet what it gives back is pure liquid savour, the backbone of Spanish rice dishes, soups, and braises. The secret is in the slow, unhurried simmer: a bubbling stock turns cloudy and bitter, while one that barely trembles will run clear and clean. Salt at the very end, for the stock will concentrate as it reduces, and an early pinch becomes an overpowering late one. This broth rewards patience the way all Spanish cooking does — with something far greater than the sum of its parts.

salt.status: ⚠ Finish salt only — do not add early
sources: salt added at end of cooking
warnings: Stock reduces significantly — early salting leads to over-salting
salt.note: As Roden notes, salt added too early becomes amplified by reduction. Season only at the finish, or leave unsalted for maximum versatility in downstream recipes.

fat.status: ✓ Moderate fat — from roasting juices and chicken skin
fat.note: The juices and fat from the roasting pan contribute deep flavor and body. Skim the surface gently while simmering to clarify the stock without stripping all its richness.

acid.status: ⚠ No acid present — consider a splash of white wine or wine vinegar
warnings: No acidic element to brighten or extract minerals from bones
acid.note: A tablespoon of white wine vinegar or dry white wine added to the cold water at the start draws more collagen and minerals from the carcass, producing a fuller, more gelatinous stock. The acid largely cooks off and does not make the stock taste sour.

heat.status: ✓ Gentle simmer — critical for clarity
heat.note: A rolling boil emulsifies fat into the liquid and turns stock cloudy and bitter. Maintain a bare shimmer — barely a bubble — for the duration of cooking. This is the difference between a murky potful and a jewel-clear caldo.

science_notes.topic: Collagen Conversion — Why Long, Slow Cooking Builds Body
science_notes.body: Chicken bones and cartilage are rich in collagen, a tough structural protein. Above 160°F (71°C) sustained for 2–3 hours, collagen denatures into gelatin, which dissolves into the liquid and creates body and a silky, lip-coating texture. This is why a well-made stock sets to a jelly when refrigerated.
science_notes.applied: Simmer for at least 2 hours to ensure adequate collagen-to-gelatin conversion. The carcass from a roasted bird has less collagen than raw bones — adding chicken wings (as Roden does) compensates for this.

science_notes.topic: Gentle Heat Preserves Clarity — The Physics of Turbidity
science_notes.body: When liquid boils vigorously, tiny fat droplets are mechanically emulsified into the water by the agitation. These suspended droplets refract light and make the stock appear grey and cloudy. At a gentle simmer, fat rises to the surface in large pools and can be skimmed away cleanly.
science_notes.applied: Keep the heat at its lowest setting after the initial scum has been removed. The surface should quiver, not boil. Skimming every 20 minutes for the first hour removes the grey proteins that rise early.

science_notes.topic: Why You Skim the Scum — Denatured Proteins and Bitterness
science_notes.body: When proteins in blood and lean muscle first hit hot water, they coagulate into grey foam that rises to the surface. These denatured proteins, if left in the pot, create astringency and off-flavors in the finished stock.
science_notes.applied: Bring the stock to a rapid boil initially to drive the scum up quickly, then immediately reduce to a simmer and skim thoroughly. This 10-minute start-and-skim process makes a cleaner, more neutral-tasting stock.

technique_notes.intro: Starting in cold water gradually dissolves proteins and minerals, drawing them out of the bones more completely than a hot-water start. The initial boil drives impurities to the surface where they can be removed.

rows.value: Break carcass into pieces to expose more marrow. Include wings and all roasting juices.
Start in cold water — not hot. This draws proteins out gradually rather than seizing them.
Once the boil is reached, foam aggressively — skim grey scum with a ladle every few minutes until clear.
If you do not skim thoroughly, the stock will taste bitter and look grey regardless of later technique.

technique_notes.intro: The long, quiet simmer extracts gelatin from the bones and marrow, building body and a clean, savoury depth that cannot be rushed.

rows.value: Surface should show occasional lazy bubbles only — never a rolling boil.
Add carrot, onion, and celery after skimming. Their sugars and aromatics contribute sweetness and round the stock's flavor.
Boiling vigorously emulsifies fat and proteins into the liquid — the result is cloudy, greasy stock that cannot be clarified.
Stock should taste round and clean, with a hint of sweetness from carrot and a gentle savoury depth.

technique_notes.intro: Straining and correct final salting transforms a good simmer into a versatile foundation stock.

rows.value: Strain through a fine-mesh sieve lined with cheesecloth for maximum clarity.
Season only after straining and reducing to final volume. Early salt concentrates to overpowering levels.
Chill overnight; lift the solid fat cap off the surface for a cleaner, clearer stock.
Refrigerate up to 4 days, or freeze in ice cube trays for recipe-ready portions.

complete.label: Classic Mirepoix ✦
complete.body: The French mirepoix trinity — carrot, onion, celery — is the foundational aromatic trio of Western stocks. Each contributes differently: onion gives sulphurous depth, carrot sweetens and colours, celery adds a bitter-green mineral note. Together they create a balanced, neutral aromatic backdrop that amplifies the chicken without competing with it.
complete.source: The Flavor Bible — Karen Page & Andrew Dornenburg

partial.label: Partial — add leek for a deeper vegetal base
partial.body: Adding leek to the mirepoix introduces a sweeter, more complex onion note. Leek is frequently used in the French version of this stock and adds a gentle allium sweetness that complements the carrot beautifully.
partial.source: On Food and Cooking — Harold McGee

flavor_combinations.note: A Parmesan rind simmered with the stock adds a quiet, savory umami depth through glutamates without altering the stock's neutral character. This technique is common in Italian kitchens but works beautifully in Spanish ones too.

Parsley stems (not leaves) and bay leaf are the quintessential Spanish aromatic pair for stock. The stems have more flavor than the leaves and add a clean, bright green note that keeps the stock light and fresh rather than heavy.

Chicken feet are extraordinarily collagen-rich. Adding just two or three to the pot produces a stock that sets firmly to a quivering jelly when cold — an indicator of deep, full-bodied gelatin content. They are inexpensive and widely available.

enhancements.title: Include the golden roasting juices from the pan — they carry Maillard compounds that cold water alone cannot create
enhancements.body: Roden specifies the juices and fat from the roasting pan, and this is not a small detail. The caramelized drippings in a roasting pan are concentrated Maillard reaction products — hundreds of flavor compounds formed when proteins and sugars were exposed to high oven heat. Dissolved into the stock, they add a roasted, golden depth that a stock made from raw bones simply cannot replicate.
enhancements.how: Deglaze the still-warm roasting pan with a cup of water before adding to the stockpot. Scrape up all the browned bits. Do not discard the fat — it will be skimmed at the simmering stage but contributes flavor before then.
sources: Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat — Samin Nosrat, heat and Maillard principles

enhancements.title: Salt only after straining and reducing — taste the finished stock before adding any seasoning
enhancements.body: Roden's note about salting at the end is the most important instruction in this recipe. Stock reduces — sometimes dramatically — as it simmers and concentrates. Salt added at the start becomes exponentially saltier as the volume decreases. An unsalted stock is infinitely versatile; a correctly salted one is a finished product; an oversalted one cannot be saved.
enhancements.how: Leave the stock completely unsalted until after straining and reducing to the desired final volume. Taste with a clean spoon and add salt gradually in small pinches. If you plan to reduce the stock further for sauces, leave it under-seasoned.
sources: Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat — Samin Nosrat, salt principles

enhancements.title: Add a splash of white wine vinegar at the cold-water start to maximize gelatin extraction
enhancements.body: A tablespoon of white wine vinegar added to the cold water creates a mildly acidic environment that helps break down collagen in the carcass bones more efficiently. Harold McGee notes that acids accelerate collagen dissolution, producing a richer, more gelatinous stock. The acid itself largely evaporates during the long simmer and does not make the finished stock taste sour. The result is a stock with noticeably more body and a silkier mouthfeel.
enhancements.how: Add 1 tablespoon of white wine vinegar or a dry sherry splash to the cold water before you begin heating. Let the bones soak for 15 minutes before turning on the heat. No other adjustment needed.
sources: On Food and Cooking — Harold McGee, collagen chemistry
Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat — Samin Nosrat, acid principles

enhancements.title: Toast the onion halves directly over a gas flame or under the broiler before adding to the pot
enhancements.body: A Spanish caldo benefits enormously from a charred onion half. The blackened exterior adds a faint bitterness and smokiness that gives the stock complexity and a rich amber color. This is a traditional technique used in Spanish cocido and many French fonds bruns. It mimics the roasted flavor of a dark stock without requiring a second round of roasting.
enhancements.how: Halve the onion and hold each half directly over a gas flame (or place cut-side-down under a broiler) until deeply caramelized — almost blackened in spots. Add directly to the cold water. The charred skin is fine to include.
sources: Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat — Samin Nosrat, heat transformation

beverage_pairing.primary: Dry Manzanilla sherry
beverage_pairing.why: Stocks are made to be cooked with rather than drunk alongside, but a sip of dry Manzanilla sherry alongside the soup made from this stock is a classic Spanish pairing. The saline, nutty sherry mirrors the savoury depth of the caldo without overwhelming it.

tiers.label: +1 Great
tiers.items: Manzanilla sherry, Fino sherry, dry Albariño

tiers.label: +0 Good
tiers.items: unoaked Chardonnay, dry Pinot Grigio, sparkling water with lemon

tiers.label: 0 Neutral
tiers.items: plain sparkling water, light lager

tiers.label: Avoid
tiers.items: full-bodied tannic reds — the weight and tannins overpower a delicate broth

rule.label: Match delicacy with delicacy
rule.text: A chicken stock is inherently light and neutral. Any beverage chosen should match its weight — heavy, tannic wines crush the subtle flavors entirely. When serving soups or dishes made from this stock, choose lean, mineral whites or dry sherries.

beyond.rec: Crisp Pilsner or light lager
beyond.why: A cold, effervescent pilsner is the Spanish table companion for clear soups — the bubbles cut through the warm richness of the broth and refresh the palate between spoonfuls.

beyond.rec: Chamomile or mild green tea
beyond.why: For a restorative occasion, chamomile tea shares the clean, floral herbaceous quality of the parsley and bay in the stock, making it a natural, warming accompaniment.

beyond.rec: Sparkling water with a lemon wedge
beyond.why: The simplest and most versatile pairing — carbonation brightens the palate and the citrus echoes the stock's clean, savory character without competing with it.

Roast chicken carcass → core flavour bas.name: Raw chicken backs and wings
Roast chicken carcass → core flavour bas.note: Yield slightly less Maillard depth but more collagen — add a tablespoon of neutral oil and roast briefly at 400°F before using to replicate some of the browned flavor.

Roast chicken carcass → core flavour bas.name: Turkey carcass
Roast chicken carcass → core flavour bas.note: Makes an even richer, deeper stock with more collagen. Works perfectly in any Spanish recipe calling for caldo de pollo.

Black peppercorns → background warmth.name: White peppercorns
Black peppercorns → background warmth.note: Milder and less assertive; the Spanish preference in delicate broths.

Black peppercorns → background warmth.name: Mixed peppercorns
Black peppercorns → background warmth.note: Adds complexity and slight fruity notes from pink and green peppercorns alongside the classic black.

best_times.rating: ⭐ Ideal — cold weather calls for restorative broths; root vegetables are at peak
Very good — new vegetables for a lighter, brighter stock
Good — useful for making ahead; less immediate need for warming soups
Good — stock is a year-round pantry essential regardless of season

name: Fish Stock (Caldo de pescado)
subtitle: The briny soul of Spanish seafood

enhancement.cookbook_narrative: Fish stock has one cardinal rule that chicken stock does not: do not overcook it. Where a chicken caldo rewards two or three hours of patient simmering, a fish caldo turns bitter and harsh after forty-five minutes — the delicate proteins of the bones breaking down not into richness but into astringency. Roden's recipe leans wisely on crustacean shells — shrimp, lobster, or crab — which produce a stock with extraordinary sweetness and depth, a subtle perfume of the sea rather than a fishy shout. Use only white-fleshed fish bones; the oily fish that Roden rightly forbids (salmon, sardines, tuna) would overwhelm every soup and sauce this stock supports. This is a subtle, silvery liquid — an invitation to the ocean rather than a plunge into it.

sources: salt added at end of cooking
warnings: Fish stock reduces and concentrates quickly — salt at the end only
salt.note: Fish stock is typically made in smaller volumes than chicken stock and reduces faster. Season only after straining, tasting for a clean briny note — not a fishy one. If it tastes aggressively fishy, the bones were overcooked.

fat.status: ⚠ Very low fat — this is intentional for fish stock
fat.note: Fish stock should be light and lean. Avoid adding any butter or oil during the simmer. Fat can be added to the final dish using this stock as a base.

acid.status: ⚠ No acid present — a splash of dry white wine is traditional
warnings: Lacks acidic element for brightness and extraction
acid.note: A 125ml glass of dry white wine (Albariño, Manzanilla) added with the cold water is the classic Spanish approach. The acid brightens the finished stock and draws minerals from the shells. The alcohol cooks off entirely.

heat.status: ✓ Short, gentle simmer — timing is critical
heat.note: Fish stock requires only 25–40 minutes of gentle simmering — far less than any other stock. Overcooking beyond 45 minutes produces a bitter, iodine-sharp liquid. The shorter the simmer, the sweeter and cleaner the result.

science_notes.topic: Why Fish Stock Must Not Be Overcooked — The Iodine Threshold
science_notes.body: Fish bones contain iodine-rich proteins that, when cooked beyond 45 minutes, begin breaking down into sulfurous and iodine compounds. These give an unpleasant, harsh 'fishy' character to the stock. Unlike collagen-heavy meat stocks, fish bones have little to gain from prolonged cooking — their gelatin extracts quickly, and the flavor peaks well before the 45-minute mark.
science_notes.applied: Set a timer. Remove the pot from heat at 35–40 minutes. Do not push to an hour hoping for more flavor — the stock will only deteriorate. Strain immediately when done.

science_notes.topic: Crustacean Shells — Chitin, Carotenoids, and Sweetness
science_notes.body: Shrimp, lobster, and crab shells contain chitin (a structural fiber), carotenoid pigments (producing the red-orange color), and a range of volatile flavor compounds that are soluble in both water and fat. When simmered, they release a sweeter, more complex flavor than white fish bones alone — one reason Roden rates them more highly.
science_notes.applied: When using shrimp shells, sauté them briefly in a dry pan or with a drop of olive oil before adding water. This brief dry heat triggers Maillard browning in the shell proteins and caramelizes the shell sugars, producing a much more complex, sweet base for the stock.

science_notes.topic: Why Oily Fish Ruins Stock — Polyunsaturated Fats and Rancidity
science_notes.body: Oily fish like salmon and sardines contain high concentrations of polyunsaturated omega-3 fats. These fats oxidize rapidly when heated in water, producing aldehydes, ketones, and short-chain fatty acids with distinctly unpleasant, fishy-rancid aromas. Even a small amount of oily fish bones contaminates an entire stockpot.
science_notes.applied: Roden's prohibition on oily fish is absolute and correct. If uncertain about the species of bones available at the fishmonger, ask for white-fleshed flatfish (sole, plaice, flounder) or round white fish (cod, hake) specifically.

temps: Cold rinse: 5 minutes under running water
technique_notes.intro: Fish bones must be thoroughly rinsed to remove blood, which creates off-flavors and a cloudy, grey stock. This preparation step is as important as anything that happens over the heat.

rows.value: Rinse bones and heads under cold running water for at least 5 minutes. Rinse until the water runs clear.
Remove the gills from any fish heads — they are bitter and cloudy. Cut them out with scissors.
Unrinsed bloody fish bones produce a dark, bitter, muddy stock that no amount of simmering will correct.
Beginning with cold water extracts more collagen from bones before heat sets proteins.

technique_notes.intro: The most disciplined moment in fish stock-making is knowing when to stop. The stock is ready when it tastes clean, sweet, and gently marine — not fishy, sharp, or bitter.

rows.value: Bring to a gentle simmer, skimming any grey foam from the surface in the first 10 minutes.
Set a timer for 35 minutes. Taste at 30 minutes for target flavor profile.
Simmering beyond 45 minutes destroys the stock. Even 5 minutes too long makes a noticeable difference.
Clean, sweet marine flavor with no sharp iodine or fishy notes. Light golden color.

partial.label: Partial — add dry white wine for classic Spanish seafood base
partial.body: The classic Spanish seafood stock includes a glass of dry Albariño or other crisp local white wine. The wine's acidity brightens the stock and the alcohol — which cooks off completely — carries aromatic compounds that water alone cannot dissolve.
partial.source: What to Drink with What You Eat — Dornenburg & Page, regional pairings

flavor_combinations.note: Adding leek to this stock introduces a sweeter, more complex allium note than onion alone, and the standard addition of 125ml dry white wine provides both acid and aromatic complexity. This is the French court-bouillon approach transplanted into Spanish waters.

Briefly sautéing shrimp shells in a film of olive oil before adding water triggers Maillard browning in the shell proteins, producing a dramatically richer, more complex flavor base. This extra 5 minutes transforms a delicate stock into something close to a Spanish bisque base.

enhancements.title: Briefly sauté shrimp or prawn shells in olive oil before adding water
enhancements.body: This is the single most impactful thing you can do to a crustacean-based stock. A two-minute sauté in a dry or lightly oiled pan triggers Maillard browning in the chitin proteins and carotenoid pigments of the shells, creating entirely new flavor compounds — toasty, nutty, and complex. The stock changes color from pale pink to a warm terracotta orange, and the flavor deepens dramatically.
enhancements.how: Heat a thin film of olive oil in the stockpot over high heat. Add the shells and cook, stirring, for 2–3 minutes until they turn pink-orange and begin to smell toasty. Do not let them burn. Add cold water immediately to deglaze, scraping up any browned bits.
sources: Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat — Samin Nosrat, heat and Maillard principles

enhancements.title: Add a glass of dry Albariño or Manzanilla at the cold-water start
enhancements.body: Dry white wine is the missing element in this recipe. Its tartaric and malic acids extract minerals and proteins from the bones more efficiently than water alone, while its aromatic compounds — apple, citrus, mineral notes from Albariño — dissolve into the stock and add a characteristic Galician quality. The alcohol evaporates entirely during cooking.
enhancements.how: Add 125ml of dry Albariño, Manzanilla sherry, or other dry Spanish white wine to the cold water before heating. This is the final formula: cold water + wine + bones + aromatics → 35-minute gentle simmer.
sources: What to Drink with What You Eat — Dornenburg & Page
The Flavor Bible — regional affinity, white wine + fish

beverage_pairing.primary: Dry Albariño (Galicia)
beverage_pairing.why: Albariño from Rías Baixas in Galicia is the natural partner to this stock and any dish built from it. The wine shares the stock's briny, mineral, ocean-adjacent quality — a regional Think Regionally match of the highest order.

tiers.label: +2 Ethereal
tiers.items: Albariño (Rías Baixas), Manzanilla sherry, Muscadet

tiers.label: +1 Great
tiers.items: Txakoli, Vermentino, Chablis, dry Pinot Grigio

tiers.label: 0 Good
tiers.items: crisp lager, dry Prosecco, sparkling water with lemon

tiers.label: Avoid
tiers.items: oaked Chardonnay, tannic reds — both overwhelm delicate fish flavors

rule.label: Think Regionally
rule.text: Albariño comes from the same Galician coast where the best fish bones originate. Regional pairings work because the wine evolved alongside the food — both shaped by the same saline Atlantic air.

beyond.rec: Crisp Spanish lager (Estrella Galicia)
beyond.why: Galicia's own lager, brewed in Santiago de Compostela, is an excellent choice — its mild bitterness and low carbonation complement a clear fish broth without competing.

beyond.rec: Mild white tea or jasmine green tea
beyond.why: The delicate, floral quality of white tea mirrors the subtle marine flavors of a good fish stock, making it an unexpectedly harmonious pairing for a light fish soup.

beyond.rec: Sparkling water with a squeeze of lemon and sliced cucumber
beyond.why: The mineral sparkle of good sparkling water with lemon echoes the stock's own clean, marine character and refreshes the palate between sips.

Fish bones and heads → flavour base.name: Whole tiny raw shrimps (as Roden suggests)
Fish bones and heads → flavour base.note: Roden's top recommendation — tiny shrimps produce a sweeter, more complex stock than plain fish bones and can often be bought inexpensively from Asian fishmongers.

Fish bones and heads → flavour base.name: Clam juice (bottled)
Fish bones and heads → flavour base.note: A ready-made shortcut: dilute 1:2 with water for a fast, clean seafood base that works well in Spanish rice dishes and fideuà.

Parsley stems → aromatic freshness.name: Fennel fronds
Parsley stems → aromatic freshness.note: Add an anise note that is particularly beautiful in fish soups and Spanish fideos. Use in the same quantity as parsley.

Parsley stems → aromatic freshness.name: Fresh dill sprigs
Parsley stems → aromatic freshness.note: A Scandinavian touch that works surprisingly well in delicate white fish stocks — bright, grassy, and mineral.

best_times.rating: ⭐ Ideal — cold-water white fish at peak; hearty fish stews in season
Very good — pre-summer seafood availability strong
Good — shellfish season at peak; great for crustacean-based stock
Good — frozen bones and shells always available

name: Spanish Vinaigrette (Vinagreta)
subtitle: Oil, vinegar, and a hard-boiled egg's quiet genius

enhancement.cookbook_narrative: This vinagreta is not the French vinaigrette it resembles. The hard-boiled egg is the Spanish signature — chopped fine, it brings a richness and a creamy, fatty presence that keeps the dressing clinging to vegetables and fish in a way that oil alone cannot. Sherry vinegar is the soul of the dressing, with its complex, nutty sweetness that makes a simple garden salad taste like something architectural. Add the Pedro Ximénez wine that Roden suggests and the vinagreta moves into Moorish territory — a faint sweetness balanced by sharp vinegar, the same dialectic of opposites that runs through all great Andalusian cooking. The parsley and red onion keep everything honest, their rawness preventing the richness of egg and olive oil from becoming heavy.

salt.status: ✓ Correctly seasoned — salt and naturally salty capers optional
salt.note: Taste before adding capers or olives — each brings significant salt. Balance accordingly. Season the dressing before tasting the finished dish with it.

fat.status: ✓ Generously fat — olive oil plus egg yolk emulsifier
fat.note: The chopped hard-boiled egg yolk acts as a natural emulsifier. When whisked with the vinegar, it helps bind the oil and acid into a more cohesive dressing that coats vegetables more evenly than a simple oil-and-vinegar separation.

acid.status: ✓ Acid present — sherry or wine vinegar
sources: 30ml sherry vinegar or white/red wine vinegar
acid.note: Sherry vinegar (especially with a splash of Pedro Ximénez) is the most complex and appropriate choice here. The standard 3:1 oil-to-vinegar ratio is correct. For more brightness, increase the vinegar by 5ml and taste.

heat.status: ✓ No heat required — raw dressing
heat.note: This is a raw dressing — no cooking required. The flavors develop as it rests. Allow at least 10 minutes after mixing for the onion to mellow slightly in the acid and for the flavors to meld.

science_notes.topic: Emulsification — Why the Egg Holds the Dressing Together
science_notes.body: Egg yolk contains lecithin, a phospholipid molecule with one water-loving and one fat-loving end. When whisked between oil and vinegar, lecithin surrounds tiny oil droplets and prevents them from coalescing back into a separate layer. This is the same chemistry that makes hollandaise and mayonnaise possible.
science_notes.applied: Chop the hard-boiled egg very fine or grate it — the more surface area of yolk that contacts the vinegar, the more lecithin is released and the more stable the emulsion. Whisk the vinegar and egg first, then add oil in a thin stream.

science_notes.topic: Sherry Vinegar — The Chemistry of Solera Aging
science_notes.body: Sherry vinegar is produced through the same solera aging system as sherry wine — successive barrels from youngest to oldest, blending over years. This process concentrates organic acids and develops complex volatile compounds including acetals, esters, and aldehyde notes. The result is a vinegar with the depth of a condiment rather than a simple acid.
science_notes.applied: Use the best sherry vinegar you can find — the label should say 'Vinagre de Jerez' (from the Jerez region). The Pedro Ximénez addition Roden suggests counters the vinegar's sharpness with raisin sweetness, creating a dressing closer to an aged balsamic in character.

technique_notes.intro: The order of assembly matters. Start with the acid and egg, then add oil in a thin stream to build the emulsion gradually.

rows.value: Whisk vinegar, salt, and finely chopped egg together until the yolk has dissolved somewhat into the vinegar.
Add olive oil gradually, whisking continuously, to create a more stable emulsion.
Let the dressing rest 10 minutes before serving — onion mellows and flavors meld.
Dressing made just before serving with raw, harsh onion will taste unbalanced. Rest time is essential.

technique_notes.intro: The final adjustment of a vinaigrette is where the cook's palate matters most.

rows.value: Dissolve salt in vinegar before adding oil — it will not dissolve in fat.
Classic French ratio is 3 parts oil : 1 part vinegar; Spanish tends toward 4:1 for a softer finish.
Add a drop more vinegar — usually insufficient acid, not insufficient salt.
A pinch of sugar or a few drops of honey rounds sharp wine vinegar without adding sweetness.

complete.label: Classic Spanish vinaigrette trio ✦
complete.body: Olive oil, sherry vinegar, and flat-leaf parsley form the classic Spanish dressing base. The oil carries flavor, the vinegar brightens, and the parsley adds green freshness and a slight bitter note that prevents the dressing from becoming heavy.
complete.source: The Food of Spain — Claudia Roden; The Flavor Bible

partial.label: Partial — add capers to complete the classic Spanish tapa dressing
partial.body: Capers would complete a vinagreta that echoes the flavors of a Niçoise-style salad — briny, sharp, and intensely savory. Roden notes capers as an optional addition; including them transforms a dressing into a condiment.
partial.source: The Flavor Bible — anchovies, capers pairings

flavor_combinations.note: Adding a tablespoon of PX sherry introduces raisin and dried-fruit sweetness that counterbalances the sharpness of the vinegar beautifully. This is the Andalusian agridulce (sweet-sour) tradition in miniature — sweet wine and sharp vinegar, a dialectic that traces back to Moorish Al-Andalus.

A single finely minced anchovy fillet added to the dressing transforms it into something close to a Caesar dressing base. The anchovy dissolves completely into the oil and adds a quiet, salty umami backbone that makes the dressing cling to vegetables and fish with new authority.

A half-teaspoon of orange blossom honey added to the dressing creates a quintessentially Andalusian balance — sweet, floral, acidic — that works beautifully as a dressing for bitter greens, grilled fish, or roasted peppers.

enhancements.title: Use aged sherry vinegar (Vinagre de Jerez Reserva) for dramatically more complexity
enhancements.body: Not all sherry vinegar is equal. A basic Vinagre de Jerez has aged 6 months; a Gran Reserva may have aged 10 years or more. The aged version has the concentrated, nutty complexity of a fine balsamic and transforms a simple dressing into something worth eating on its own. The price difference is significant but the impact on any Spanish vinagreta is transformative.
enhancements.how: Substitute standard sherry vinegar 1:1 with a Reserva or Gran Reserva. Reduce the volume by 20% to account for its greater intensity. Taste before adding the Pedro Ximénez — you may not need the sweetener at all.
sources: The Flavor Thesaurus — Niki Segnit, vinegar entries; Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat — Samin Nosrat, acid

enhancements.title: Add capers and chopped black olives to transform this vinagreta into a full tapa sauce
enhancements.body: Roden lists capers and olives as optional additions. Together, they transform a simple dressing into a full tapa condiment — briny, salty, and intensely savory. This expanded vinagreta is excellent with boiled vegetables, grilled fish, and canned tuna. The capers should be rinsed if salt-packed.
enhancements.how: Add 15ml of rinsed capers (chopped if large) and 30ml of finely chopped black olives. Stir into the finished dressing. Taste for salt before adding more — both capers and olives are naturally salty.
sources: The Flavor Bible — anchovy, caper, olive pairings

beverage_pairing.primary: Fino or Manzanilla sherry
beverage_pairing.why: The nutty, saline character of Fino and Manzanilla mirrors the sherry vinegar in the dressing. Served chilled alongside boiled vegetables or grilled fish dressed with this vinagreta, the match is impeccable.

tiers.label: +1 Great
tiers.items: Fino sherry, Manzanilla sherry, Albariño

tiers.label: +0 Good
tiers.items: dry Verdejo, crisp Sauvignon Blanc, Txakoli

tiers.label: 0 Neutral
tiers.items: light lager, sparkling water with lemon

tiers.label: Avoid
tiers.items: sweet wines — sweetness clashes with the vinegar's intentional acidity; heavy oaked whites dull the dressing's brightness

rule.label: Mirror the dominant acid
rule.text: This dressing is built on sherry vinegar. Matching the wine to the vinegar's own base wine (sherry → Fino/Manzanilla) creates a seamless pairing where the glass and the dish speak the same language.

beyond.rec: Crisp Spanish lager or Estrella Damm
beyond.why: Light, cold lager cuts through the olive oil richness and complements the sharpness of the vinegar without competing — the standard tapas bar companion.

beyond.rec: Cold-brewed green tea with lemon
beyond.why: The light tannins in cold-brewed green tea contrast the oil in the dressing while lemon bridges to the vinegar's acidity — an elegant non-alcoholic pairing.

beyond.rec: Sparkling water with a slice of orange
beyond.why: The orange's volatile aromatics echo the slight fruity sweetness of Pedro Ximénez sherry vinegar and provide a refreshing counterpoint to the dressing's richness.

Sherry vinegar → acid backbone.name: White wine vinegar
Sherry vinegar → acid backbone.note: Sharper and more neutral than sherry vinegar — add a small pinch of sugar to approximate the nutty sweetness. A perfectly acceptable substitution.

Sherry vinegar → acid backbone.name: Lemon juice
Sherry vinegar → acid backbone.note: Brighter and more citric than vinegar — creates a lighter, fresher-tasting dressing. Excellent for fish and seafood.

Hard-boiled egg → emulsifier and richnes.name: Extra egg yolk (raw)
Hard-boiled egg → emulsifier and richnes.note: A raw egg yolk whisked in creates a much more stable, creamier emulsion closer to a light mayonnaise. Use immediately.

Hard-boiled egg → emulsifier and richnes.name: Dijon mustard (1 tsp)
Hard-boiled egg → emulsifier and richnes.note: The vegan alternative — Dijon contains emulsifying mucilage that creates a stable dressing without egg. Adds a mustardy sharpness.

best_times.rating: ⭐ Ideal — serve over grilled summer vegetables and fresh fish
Very good — early vegetables and grilled asparagus benefit greatly
Good — pairs well with roasted peppers and autumn salads
Good — a bright acid note lifts winter root vegetable dishes

name: Tomato Vinaigrette (Vinagreta de tomate)
subtitle: Catalonia's sun-ripe tomato dressing

enhancement.cookbook_narrative: This Catalan dressing is summer on a plate — ripe tomatoes, lemon or wine vinegar, and good olive oil, nothing more. In Catalonia it dresses boiled vegetables and steamed seafood with a confidence that belies its simplicity. The tomato here is not a background note but the flavor itself: it should be so ripe that it barely needs cutting, bleeding juice the moment a knife touches it. The choice between lemon juice and wine vinegar changes the dressing's personality entirely — lemon makes it brighter and more Mediterranean, vinegar gives it an earthier, more inland character. Use this vinagreta on anything that needs waking up.

salt.note: Season generously — tomatoes need salt to release their full sweetness and balance their natural acidity.

fat.status: ✓ Extra virgin olive oil — the dominant flavor
fat.note: Use the best olive oil you have for this dressing. Since there are so few ingredients, the quality of each one is magnified. A grassy, peppery Catalan olive oil is ideal.

acid.status: ✓ Acid present — vinegar or lemon
acid.note: Fresh lemon juice creates a brighter, citrus-forward dressing; wine vinegar creates a more complex, earthy one. Use lemon for fish, vinegar for meat or cooked vegetables.

heat.status: ✓ No heat — raw preparation
heat.note: No cooking required. However, the dressing improves dramatically if the tomatoes are at room temperature and perfectly ripe.

science_notes.topic: Tomato Ripeness and Glutamate Content
science_notes.body: Ripe tomatoes contain higher concentrations of glutamic acid, the amino acid responsible for savory umami flavor, than underripe ones. As tomatoes ripen, starch converts to sugars and acids, and glutamate levels increase sharply. A fully ripe tomato is not just sweeter — it is fundamentally more savory.
science_notes.applied: Only use tomatoes at peak ripeness, ideally vine-ripened and at room temperature. Refrigeration suppresses the volatile aromatic compounds responsible for tomato flavor — take them out of the fridge at least an hour before making this dressing.

science_notes.topic: Why Salt Transforms Tomatoes
science_notes.body: Salt draws water out of tomato cells through osmosis, concentrating the flavor compounds and softening the texture. In a dressing, lightly salted diced tomato releases additional juice that becomes part of the emulsion, creating a more integrated, cohesive dressing than unsalted tomato.
science_notes.applied: Dice the tomatoes, salt them lightly, and let them drain for 5 minutes before assembling the dressing. The juice they release can be whisked with the vinegar and olive oil for a more unified, intense flavor.

technique_notes.intro: The quality of the tomato is everything here. Preparation should enhance its natural flavor, not diminish it.

rows.value: Use only tomatoes that yield to gentle pressure. Unripe tomatoes produce a harsh, astringent dressing.
Room temperature tomatoes have more accessible volatile aromatics than cold ones. Never make this with refrigerated tomatoes.
Salt diced tomatoes lightly, let drain for 5 minutes, then add the drained juice to the dressing base.
Dice or chop — do not purée. Texture is part of this dressing's identity.

technique_notes.intro: Tomato vinaigrette achieves its characteristic body through careful emulsification of tomato pulp and oil.

rows.value: Add oil last, in a thin stream, whisking constantly to build a stable emulsion.
Straining through a fine sieve after blending produces a silkier, restaurant-quality result.
Season after emulsification is complete — salt draws water from tomato and can break the emulsion if added early.
Use within 24 hours; natural pectin in tomato degrades and the dressing will separate irreversibly.

complete.label: Tomato + olive oil + acid ✦
complete.body: The fundamental Mediterranean flavor triad: tomato, olive oil, acid. These three ingredients share flavor compounds (particularly aldehydes and esters) that make them naturally synergistic. The combination is older than any recipe — it is the foundation of Mediterranean cuisine.
complete.source: The Flavor Bible — tomato essential pairings

flavor_combinations.note: A pinch of anchovy paste dissolved into the olive oil adds umami depth that amplifies the tomato's own savory quality without adding any fishiness. A classic Catalan trick.

Torn basil added just before serving adds the classic Mediterranean aromatic bridge between tomato and olive oil. Basil and tomato share flavor compounds — they are one of the most scientifically confirmed flavor affinities in cuisine.

enhancements.title: Peel and seed the tomatoes for a silkier, more refined dressing
enhancements.body: Blanched and peeled tomatoes lose their tough skin, which can dominate the texture of the dressing. Seeding removes excess liquid and the sharp acidic cores, leaving only the sweet flesh. The resulting dressing has a more refined texture that clings beautifully to fish and seafood.
enhancements.how: Score the base of each tomato with a cross, blanch in boiling water for 30 seconds, shock in ice water, peel, halve, and scoop out seeds before dicing.
sources: The Food of Spain — Claudia Roden technique notes

enhancements.title: Add half a teaspoon of orange blossom honey for Catalan agridulce depth
enhancements.body: A small amount of honey bridges the natural sweetness of ripe tomatoes with the sharp vinegar, creating a dressing with a pleasant balance of sweet and sour that is quintessentially Catalan. This is particularly good with oily fish like grilled sardines or mackerel.
enhancements.how: Whisk 2–3ml of honey into the vinegar before adding the oil. Taste and adjust — the honey should be barely perceptible, a background sweetness rather than a dominant flavor.
sources: The Flavor Bible — tomato + honey pairing notes

beverage_pairing.primary: Dry Catalan Cava or Albariño
beverage_pairing.why: Dry Cava from Penedès matches the dressing's acid and the light, summery character of the dishes it typically dresses. The bubbles cut through olive oil richness cleanly.

tiers.label: +1 Great
tiers.items: Dry Cava (Penedès), Albariño, Txakoli

tiers.label: 0 Good
tiers.items: Sauvignon Blanc, dry Pinot Grigio, light rosé

tiers.label: 0 Neutral
tiers.items: light lager, sparkling water

tiers.label: Avoid
tiers.items: oaked Chardonnay, heavy reds — overwhelm a delicate fresh-tomato dressing

rule.label: Think Regionally — Catalan Cava
rule.text: Catalan Cava is produced within miles of where this vinagreta was born. The regional match is perfect — both are built on fresh acidity and Mediterranean sunshine.

beyond.rec: Estrella Damm (Barcelona) or crisp lager
beyond.why: Light, cold lager balances the acid in the dressing without competing; Estrella Damm's Catalan origin makes it a natural regional choice.

beyond.rec: Cold-brewed tomato leaf tea or green tea
beyond.why: A novelty but a genuine one — tomato leaves brewed cold produce a subtle, herbaceous liquid that reflects the garden character of this simple dressing.

beyond.rec: Horchata de chufa (Valencian tiger nut milk)
beyond.why: The faintly sweet, earthy horchata echoes this dressing's simplicity and is deeply regional to the Spanish Mediterranean coast.

Fresh tomatoes → core flavor.name: Quality tinned whole plum tomatoes (drained, seeded)
Fresh tomatoes → core flavor.note: Out of season, drain and seed good San Marzano tinned tomatoes. Pat dry well — tinned tomatoes have more water than fresh. The flavor is different but still good.

Fresh tomatoes → core flavor.name: Cherry tomatoes, halved
Fresh tomatoes → core flavor.note: Sweeter and more intensely flavored than large tomatoes. No need to peel. Particularly good in summer when cherry tomatoes are at peak.

Wine vinegar/lemon → acid.name: Sherry vinegar
Wine vinegar/lemon → acid.note: More complex and nutty than wine vinegar — changes the dressing's character from bright and light to deeper and more autumnal.

Wine vinegar/lemon → acid.name: Pomegranate molasses (1 tsp)
Wine vinegar/lemon → acid.note: Adds a sweet-sour depth and a fruity-tart quality that pairs beautifully with tomatoes and oily fish.

best_times.rating: ⭐ Ideal — tomatoes at peak ripeness and sweetness
Very good — late summer tomatoes still excellent
Good — use hothouse tomatoes but choose the ripest
Not ideal — substitute good tinned tomatoes

name: Tomato and Onion Dressing (Salmorreta)
subtitle: Valencian fire — a dressing that doubles as a sauce

enhancement.cookbook_narrative: The salmorreta is the working dressing of the Valencian coast — unrefined, generous, deliberately rough-edged. What distinguishes it from a simple vinaigrette is the chile pepper, which introduces a flicker of heat that makes everything else taste more alive. Roden's note about broiling the tomato is the kind of detail that separates a cook who has actually tasted from one who has only read: the charred skin and concentrated sugars of a broiled tomato transform this from a salad dressing into something that belongs alongside grilled fish like an argument. Raw or cooked, it speaks Valencian: loud, sunny, uncompromising, with the olive oil holding everything together like the sea holds a coast.

salt.note: The chile pepper already adds piquancy — season with restraint and taste before adding salt.

fat.status: ✓ Generous olive oil
fat.note: The oil is the carrier here — it picks up the volatile aromatics of the chile, onion, and parsley and distributes them evenly through the dressing.

acid.status: ✓ Wine vinegar present
acid.note: The acid balances the sweetness of the onion and the heat of the chile. Use red wine vinegar for depth or white wine vinegar for brightness.

heat.status: ⚠ Optional — broiling the tomato adds roasted complexity
heat.note: Roden's tip to broil the tomato is significant. The char adds bitterness and smokiness that balance the chile's heat and the onion's sweetness — a dimension the raw version lacks.

science_notes.topic: Maillard Browning in Broiled Tomatoes
science_notes.body: When the tomato is broiled until the skin blisters, the sugars and amino acids on the surface undergo Maillard reactions above 150°C, creating dozens of new flavor compounds — nutty, caramel-like, and slightly bitter. The charred skin also contributes phenolic compounds with a faint smokiness.
science_notes.applied: Place the whole tomato under a hot broiler and turn every few minutes until the skin blisters and blackens in spots. Remove, cool slightly, then chop skin and all. The blackened skin pieces add depth without bitterness if used in moderation.

science_notes.topic: Chile Pepper Heat — Capsaicin and Fat Solubility
science_notes.body: Capsaicin, the compound responsible for chile heat, is not water-soluble — it dissolves in fat. This means the olive oil in this dressing carries and amplifies the heat from the chile throughout the entire preparation, creating a more even distribution of spice than water-based preparations.
science_notes.applied: Finely chop the chile and add directly to the olive oil first, letting it infuse for 5 minutes before adding other ingredients. This ensures even heat distribution and prevents hot spots of concentrated capsaicin.

technique_notes.stage: Stage 1 — Broiling the Tomato (Optional but Recommended)
technique_notes.intro: The broiled tomato version produces a dramatically more complex dressing. This extra step takes 10 minutes and changes everything.

rows.value: Place whole tomato on foil-lined tray. Broil on maximum heat, turning every 3 minutes.
Skin should be fully blistered and charred in spots. Tomato should be soft inside.
Allow to cool for 5 minutes before chopping — it will be very hot and release a lot of steam.
Do not broil until completely black — some char is good, but a fully carbonized tomato makes the dressing bitter.

technique_notes.intro: Salmorreta's power lies in its deep concentration — the finishing steps lock in that intensity.

rows.value: Finished paste should hold a mounded shape on a spoon — add a few drops of liquid if it seizes.
Tomato must be cooked until almost completely dry; residual water causes the paste to sour quickly.
Frying garlic to deep golden (not pale) extracts bitter Maillard compounds that balance the sweet tomato.
Recipe yields roughly 4–5 tablespoons — a small quantity with enormous aromatic impact per spoonful.

partial.label: Partial — add roasted red pepper for Valencian romesco character
partial.body: Adding a roasted red pepper to this salmorreta moves it toward the romesco family of Catalan-Valencian sauces. The smoky sweetness of the pepper complements the broiled tomato's char and the chile's heat beautifully.
partial.source: The Flavor Bible — tomato + pepper + garlic affinities

flavor_combinations.note: A small amount of anchovy paste dissolved into the oil adds a savory backbone that makes this dressing taste more complete without being identifiably fishy. The Spanish already use boquerones (white anchovies) in similar contexts.
Crushed garlic is mentioned as optional but is in fact the traditional Valencian base — along with parsley and tomato, it forms the sofregit trifecta of the eastern Spanish coast.

enhancements.title: Broil the tomato for a charred, smoky dressing — Roden's own first preference
enhancements.body: The broiled tomato version is categorically more complex than the raw version. Maillard reactions on the tomato skin create smoky, bitter compounds that balance the chile and onion, producing a dressing with the depth of a sauce. It takes ten minutes and the difference is not subtle.
enhancements.how: Broil a quartered or whole tomato until skin blisters and some char appears. Cool and chop, skin and all, retaining all juices. Proceed as per raw version.
sources: Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat — Samin Nosrat, heat transformation

enhancements.title: Char the onion quarters alongside the tomato for sweeter, more complex allium base
enhancements.body: Raw onion in a dressing has an aggressive sharpness that can dominate. Briefly charring the onion quarters under the broiler — just 5 minutes, enough to soften the exterior — mellows its sulfurous compounds while keeping its structure, producing a sweeter, more integrated base for the dressing.
enhancements.how: Add the onion pieces to the broiling tray with the tomato. Remove after 5 minutes (before the tomato is done) — enough to char the edges without fully cooking.
sources: On Food and Cooking — Harold McGee, allium chemistry

beverage_pairing.primary: Dry Valencian rosé or chilled Tempranillo
beverage_pairing.why: The spice and acid in this dressing call for a wine with enough fruit to stand against the chile's heat but light enough not to overwhelm the grilled fish it typically accompanies.

tiers.label: +1 Great
tiers.items: Dry Spanish rosé (Garnacha), lightly chilled Tempranillo, Grenache-based rosé

tiers.label: 0 Good
tiers.items: Verdejo, dry Albariño, cold lager

tiers.label: 0 Neutral
tiers.items: sparkling water, light mineral water

tiers.label: Avoid
tiers.items: tannic heavy reds — tannin amplifies chile heat to painful levels

rule.label: Spice + Tannin = Pain
rule.text: Capsaicin and tannins interact chemically to dramatically amplify each other's heat and astringency. With any chile-containing dish, avoid tannic reds. Choose fruity, low-tannin wines or cold lager instead.

beyond.rec: Cold Valencian lager or cerveza rubia
beyond.why: Cold beer is the ideal companion to spiced fish dishes — the carbonation lifts the chile heat away from the palate and the slight bitterness echoes the broiled tomato.

beyond.rec: Hibiscus iced tea
beyond.why: Hibiscus has a natural sour-fruity quality that mirrors the vinegar in the dressing and its berry-red color echoes the tomato's richness visually as well as in flavor.

beyond.rec: Agua fresca de tomate (fresh tomato water with lime and mint)
beyond.why: A blended and strained fresh tomato drink with lime and mint creates a meta-pairing that reflects the dressing's own flavors in liquid form — refreshing and completely Spanish.

Red chile pepper → heat.name: Pimentón picante (smoked hot paprika, ½ tsp)
Red chile pepper → heat.note: Substitutes heat with smoky, dried-chile character. Dissolve in the olive oil first to bloom the spice.

Red chile pepper → heat.name: Dried chili flakes (½ tsp)
Red chile pepper → heat.note: Easy substitution — same heat level without the fresh green flavor. Standard pantry option.

Wine vinegar → acid.name: Sherry vinegar
Wine vinegar → acid.note: Richer and more complex — particularly good in the broiled tomato version where its nutty depth complements the char.

Wine vinegar → acid.name: Fresh lime juice
Wine vinegar → acid.note: Lighter and more citric; works beautifully alongside the chile for a slightly more Mexican direction.

best_times.rating: ⭐ Ideal — tomatoes and chiles both at peak; perfect with grilled fish
Very good — late summer produce still excellent
Good — use tinned tomatoes and dried chiles; roasting them essential for depth

name: Green Sauce with Parsley (Mojo de perejil)
subtitle: The Canary Islands' defiant green condiment

enhancement.cookbook_narrative: The Canary Islands' mojos are among the most powerful, assertive sauces in the Spanish repertoire — nothing like the gentle vinaigrettes of the mainland. Mojo de perejil is the green one, built on a fist of parsley and as much garlic as you dare: the islands have always been more generous with garlic than the rest of Spain, perhaps because of their position at the crossroads of Africa, Europe, and the New World. The sauce is deliberately sharp and vivid — the white wine vinegar sets the tone from the first spoon, and the raw garlic provides a heat that holds a match to grilled fish, boiled potatoes, and cured meats. It keeps well in the refrigerator, Roden notes, which is true: the flavors deepen and mellow over two or three days, the garlic becoming less strident and more savory.

salt.note: Salt generously — the large volume of parsley absorbs salt and the sauce will taste under-seasoned without a good pinch.

fat.status: ✓ Generously fat —175ml mild olive oil
fat.note: The mild olive oil is specified for good reason — a very peppery, assertive oil would compete with the garlic and parsley. A light, fruity olive oil from the Canaries or Andalusia is ideal.

acid.status: ✓ Strong acid — white or red wine vinegar
acid.note: The relatively high ratio of vinegar to oil (nearly 1:4 rather than the standard 1:3) makes this sauce punchy and bright. Do not reduce the vinegar — it is intentional.

heat.status: ✓ No heat — raw preparation
heat.note: This sauce is entirely raw and benefits from being made at least an hour ahead to allow the garlic to mellow slightly in the acid.

science_notes.topic: Garlic Allicin and How Acid Transforms It
science_notes.body: Crushed garlic releases allicin, the sulfurous compound responsible for its sharp, pungent aroma. Raw allicin is extremely volatile and harsh. When garlic rests in an acidic environment (vinegar), allicin begins converting to milder, more complex sulfur compounds — diallyl disulfides and thiosulfinates — that are less sharp but more savory.
science_notes.applied: Make this mojo at least 30–60 minutes before serving. The vinegar will soften the garlic's raw edge and allow its savory depth to emerge. Made the day before, it becomes a genuinely different — and better — sauce.

science_notes.topic: Parsley Chlorophyll and How to Preserve It
science_notes.body: Parsley's vivid green color comes from chlorophyll, which breaks down rapidly when exposed to heat or acid. In a raw preparation like this mojo, the vinegar will slowly dull the color over time — the sauce may turn from brilliant green to olive-drab after 24 hours.
science_notes.applied: For the most vibrant green color, use cold water and ice to rinse the parsley before blending and add the vinegar only just before serving. If making ahead, keep the garlic-parsley-oil blend separate and add the vinegar only when ready to serve.

temps: Cold water rinse, room temperature preparation
technique_notes.intro: The leaves, not the stems, are what this mojo needs — strip them carefully and measure after rather than before for accuracy.

rows.value: Use only the leaves; thick parsley stems are woody and bitter. Strip with fingers or scissors.
Wash in cold water and dry thoroughly before blending. Excess water dilutes the sauce.
Pack loosely to measure — 'a little more than 235ml' is Roden's specification.
Undried parsley produces a watery, diluted sauce with no body.

technique_notes.intro: Traditional mojo is made in a mortar and pestle, which creates a more textured, rustic paste. A food processor creates a smoother, brighter sauce.

rows.value: Mortar for texture and tradition; food processor for speed and smoothness. Both produce excellent results.
Add olive oil gradually for a more emulsified, cohesive sauce.
Add vinegar and salt last, adjust to taste. The sauce should taste assertively of parsley and garlic.
Brilliant green color, pourable but not watery, with visible texture if mortar-made.

complete.label: Parsley + garlic + olive oil ✦
complete.body: This trio is the fundamental herb-sauce building block of Mediterranean cooking, appearing in gremolata, persillade, chimichurri, and countless other traditions. Parsley and garlic are flavor-synergistic — garlic provides the sulfurous backbone that the mild parsley builds on.
complete.narrative: Parsley and garlic share a volatile sulfur compound called di-methyl sulfide which means they reinforce each other's flavor
complete.source: The Flavor Bible — parsley essential pairings; The Flavor Thesaurus — Niki Segnit, parsley + garlic

flavor_combinations.note: The canonical Canarian pairing — wrinkly salt-crusted potatoes served with mojo verde and mojo rojo. The starchy, salty potato is the perfect neutral vehicle for the assertive sauce.

Adding a small handful of fresh cilantro alongside the parsley adds a brighter, more floral note that is actually traditional in the Canary Islands — the islands' proximity to Africa and Latin America makes cilantro a natural companion to parsley in the local kitchen.

enhancements.title: Add a small handful of cilantro alongside the parsley for Canarian authenticity
enhancements.body: The Canary Islands sit closer to Morocco than to Madrid, and cilantro (fresh coriander) appears in the island's cooking in ways not found in mainland Spain. A 30/70 blend of cilantro and parsley adds a bright, floral, slightly citrusy dimension to the mojo that reflects its true island origins.
enhancements.how: Replace one-quarter of the measured parsley with fresh cilantro. Add with the parsley and blend as normal. Taste — the cilantro flavor should be present but secondary.
sources: The Flavor Bible — parsley + cilantro complementary herbs

enhancements.title: Toast the garlic in the olive oil first for a rounder, sweeter, less strident sauce
enhancements.body: For a milder version of this mojo — one more suited to delicate fish than cured meats — gently warm the crushed garlic in the olive oil over very low heat for 5 minutes until it turns golden and fragrant. Cool completely before blending. This gentler approach transforms raw pungency into a roasted, almost nutty sweetness.
enhancements.how: Warm 175ml olive oil with 5 garlic cloves (crushed) over the lowest heat possible for 5 minutes. Do not let the garlic brown — just soften and infuse. Cool completely before blending with the parsley.
sources: Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat — Samin Nosrat, fat and heat

beverage_pairing.primary: Crisp Canarian white (Listán Blanco) or Albariño
beverage_pairing.why: The Canary Islands produce excellent indigenous whites from Listán Blanco grapes — lean, mineral, and saline in character, they are the natural partner to this assertive green sauce.

tiers.label: +1 Great
tiers.items: Canarian Listán Blanco, Albariño, dry Verdejo

tiers.label: 0 Good
tiers.items: Sauvignon Blanc, Manzanilla, Txakoli

tiers.label: 0 Neutral
tiers.items: light lager, cold sparkling water

tiers.label: Avoid
tiers.items: full-bodied oaked reds, tannic wines — the raw garlic and vinegar make tannins taste metallic and harsh

rule.label: Raw Garlic + Tannin = Metallic Clash
rule.text: Raw garlic's sulfur compounds react with wine tannins to produce a metallic, harsh flavor. Always choose low-tannin or no-tannin wines with raw-garlic preparations.

beyond.rec: Cerveza Tropical (Canarian lager)
beyond.why: The Canary Islands' own Cerveza Tropical is a pale lager brewed in Gran Canaria — the regional pairing for papas arrugadas con mojo.

beyond.rec: Cold peppermint tea with lemon
beyond.why: Mint's cooling effect is a natural counterpoint to the heat of raw garlic — refreshing and palate-cleansing between servings of this assertive sauce.

beyond.rec: Sparkling water with cucumber and mint
beyond.why: The mild, cooling cucumber echoes the fresh green quality of the parsley while mint provides a counterpoint to the garlic's sharpness.

Flat-leaf parsley → herb base.name: Cilantro / fresh coriander (partial substitution)
Flat-leaf parsley → herb base.note: Replace up to 50% with cilantro for a more assertive, floral mojo. Traditional in the Canary Islands.

Flat-leaf parsley → herb base.name: Watercress (partial substitution)
Flat-leaf parsley → herb base.note: Replaces some parsley with a peppery, mustardy bite. Produces a mojo with more heat and complexity.

Wine vinegar → acid.name: Sherry vinegar
Wine vinegar → acid.note: More complex and nutty — a small reduction in quantity is needed as it is more intense. Changes the sauce's character from sharp-green to deeper and more savory.

Wine vinegar → acid.name: Lemon juice
Wine vinegar → acid.note: Lighter and more citric. Creates a brighter mojo that is particularly good with grilled fish and seafood.

best_times.rating: ⭐ Ideal — parsley is always available; this sauce has no seasonal limitations
⭐ Ideal — serve with grilled fish and papas arrugadas
Very good — excellent with roasted meats and cured ham

name: Mayonnaise (Mayonesa)
subtitle: The Catalan emulsion that conquered the world

enhancement.cookbook_narrative: Mayonnaise was born in Spain — not France — a point of quiet pride in the Balearic Islands, where Port Mahon in Menorca gave the sauce its name. Roden's recipe returns to that origin: egg yolk, acid, oil, and little else, the French additions stripped away to reveal the clean, pure emulsion beneath. The 2:1 ratio of sunflower to mild olive oil is deliberate — pure olive oil makes a heavy, faintly bitter mayonnaise, while pure sunflower makes something bland and supermarket-like. The blend creates something that has the fruitiness of olive oil without its dominance, the lightness of sunflower without its forgettability. Temperature is the one technical non-negotiable: cold eggs do not emulsify properly, and cold oil goes into solution too slowly. Room temperature, always.

salt.status: ✓ Implicit — lemon juice or vinegar provides both acid and some saltiness
sources: lemon juice or white wine vinegar
salt.note: Season carefully — mayonnaise is eaten cold, which suppresses saltiness perception slightly. Season more assertively than you think necessary, then taste again after 5 minutes.

fat.status: ✓ Fat-dominated — oil is the entire structure
fat.note: The fat IS the mayonnaise. Every other ingredient exists only to emulsify and flavor it. The ratio and type of oils determine the final flavor more than anything else.

acid.status: ✓ Essential for emulsification and flavor
sources: 10–20ml lemon juice or white wine vinegar
acid.note: The acid has two roles: it flavors the mayonnaise and it provides a water-based phase for the emulsion. Without sufficient acid, the oil and egg yolk will not fully emulsify. Lemon juice gives brightness; wine vinegar gives depth.

heat.status: ✓ No heat — cold emulsion technique
heat.note: All ingredients must be at room temperature. Cold ingredients, especially eggs directly from the refrigerator, are the most common cause of broken mayonnaise.

science_notes.topic: Emulsification — How Lecithin Creates Mayonnaise
science_notes.body: Egg yolk contains lecithin, a phospholipid molecule that acts as an emulsifier by surrounding tiny oil droplets and preventing them from coalescing. Each egg yolk contains approximately 10g of lecithin, enough to emulsify up to 7 liters of oil — far more than any recipe requires. The emulsification is physical, not chemical: lecithin does not react with oil, it merely surrounds it.
science_notes.applied: This means you should add oil very slowly at first — the emulsion needs to be established before oil is added quickly. The first 50ml of oil should be added drop by drop. Once the emulsion is clearly forming (thick, pale, creamy), the remaining oil can be added in a thin stream.

science_notes.topic: Why Room Temperature Prevents Broken Mayonnaise
science_notes.body: Cold fat is more viscous and forms larger droplets when added to the emulsion base. Large droplets are harder for lecithin to surround, creating an unstable emulsion that breaks more easily. Room temperature fat flows into smaller droplets naturally, and room temperature egg yolks have more mobile lecithin molecules that can surround them more efficiently.
science_notes.applied: Remove eggs from the refrigerator at least 30 minutes before making mayonnaise. If oil is stored in the refrigerator (olive oil especially), warm it to room temperature first. Roden's instruction about this is not a suggestion — it is the difference between mayonnaise and an expensive bowl of broken oil.

technique_notes.intro: The first 50ml of oil added to the egg yolks establishes the emulsion. This stage is where broken mayonnaise is born or prevented.

rows.value: All ingredients at room temperature. This is non-negotiable.
Whisk egg yolks and 10ml acid together until pale and slightly thickened before adding any oil.
Add oil drop by drop for the first 50ml, whisking constantly after each drop.
Adding oil too fast in the beginning is the primary cause of broken mayonnaise. Patience here saves everything.

technique_notes.intro: Once the emulsion is established, oil can be added faster. The pace gradually increases as the emulsion becomes more stable.

rows.value: Once emulsion is clearly forming (pale, thick), increase to a thin but steady stream, whisking constantly.
Correctly emulsified mayonnaise turns pale yellow to ivory as oil is incorporated. It should look creamy and hold peaks.
If the mixture suddenly looks greasy, shiny, and separates — stop immediately. Add a teaspoon of cold water and whisk vigorously.
Add remaining acid and salt. Taste — it should be bright, creamy, and distinctly eggy.

complete.label: Egg yolk + olive oil + acid emulsion ✦
complete.body: The fundamental emulsion triad — the building block from which alioli, remoulade, tartar sauce, and a hundred other condiments descend. The chemistry here is ancient: egg lecithin, fat, and water-based acid have been combined in the Mediterranean for millennia.
complete.source: On Food and Cooking — Harold McGee; The Flavor Bible — emulsification basics

flavor_combinations.note: A tiny pinch of saffron dissolved in the lemon juice before incorporation creates a golden-hued mayonnaise with a floral, honey-like complexity. This saffron alioli is traditional in the Valencian and Catalan repertoire as an accompaniment to rice dishes.

Adding one clove of roasted garlic (mashed to a paste) to the egg yolk base before building the mayonnaise creates a sweeter, more complex garlic mayo than raw alioli — rich and nutty without the sharp bite.

enhancements.title: Use a 2:1 blend of sunflower and mild olive oil as Roden specifies — avoid 100% olive oil
enhancements.body: Pure olive oil makes a heavy, faintly bitter mayonnaise with an intensity that overwhelms delicate dishes. Pure sunflower produces something bland and characterless. Roden's 2:1 sunflower-to-olive-oil ratio is the correct technical balance: it creates a mayonnaise that is light and neutral on the palate but with underlying fruitiness and complexity from the olive component.
enhancements.how: Measure 200ml sunflower oil and 95ml mild extra virgin olive oil into the same pouring vessel. Add this blend gradually — not the pure olive oil in a single pour.
sources: Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat — Samin Nosrat, fat selection

enhancements.title: Add 5ml Dijon mustard to the egg yolk base for a more stable emulsion and a subtle French bridge
enhancements.body: Dijon mustard contains mucilage compounds that act as additional emulsifiers, making the mayonnaise dramatically more stable and forgiving. It also adds a mild, spicy depth. This is technically a French addition to what is a Spanish base, but the result is a more reliable, more forgiving emulsion.
enhancements.how: Whisk 5ml Dijon mustard into the egg yolks before adding any oil. Proceed as normal.
sources: On Food and Cooking — Harold McGee, emulsification chemistry

beverage_pairing.primary: Crisp Cava brut or cold Albariño
beverage_pairing.why: Mayonnaise's richness and fat require an acidic, cleansing beverage. Crisp Cava provides carbonation that cuts through the emulsion and acid that balances the olive oil's richness.

tiers.label: +1 Great
tiers.items: Brut Cava, dry Albariño, crisp Manzanilla

tiers.label: 0 Good
tiers.items: Sauvignon Blanc, unoaked Chardonnay, crisp lager

tiers.label: 0 Neutral
tiers.items: sparkling water with lemon

tiers.label: Avoid
tiers.items: oaked Chardonnay, full-bodied reds — fat-on-fat pairing creates a heavy, cloying effect

rule.label: Acid Cuts Fat
rule.text: Mayonnaise is essentially pure emulsified fat. High-acid wines slice through the fat coating on the tongue, refreshing the palate and preventing the dish from feeling heavy. Always choose high-acid beverages.

beyond.rec: Cold pilsner or wheat beer
beyond.why: The carbonation in beer is even more effective than wine carbonation at cutting through mayonnaise's fat. A cold wheat beer adds a gentle yeasty note that complements the egg richness.

beyond.rec: Cold black tea with lemon
beyond.why: Black tea tannins and lemon acid together work to cut the fat and refresh the palate — a traditional pairing for egg-based salads and sandwiches.

beyond.rec: Sparkling water with lemon and a pinch of salt
beyond.why: The combination of carbonation and acid mimics the effect of brut Cava while the pinch of salt echoes the seasoning in the mayonnaise.

Egg yolks → emulsifier.name: Aquafaba (chickpea water, 3 tbsp per yolk)
Egg yolks → emulsifier.note: The vegan alternative — chickpea water contains proteins and saponins that create a stable emulsion. The flavor is milder and the texture slightly less rich, but the technique is identical.

Egg yolks → emulsifier.name: Whole egg (1 egg instead of 2 yolks)
Egg yolks → emulsifier.note: A more stable but slightly less rich emulsion. Works well for beginners and for mayonnaises that will be used as a cooking ingredient rather than a condiment.

Sunflower oil → neutral fat carrier.name: Light-flavored rapeseed/canola oil
Sunflower oil → neutral fat carrier.note: Neutral in flavor, similar texture to sunflower oil. A perfectly acceptable substitution.

Sunflower oil → neutral fat carrier.name: Grapeseed oil
Sunflower oil → neutral fat carrier.note: Very light, almost flavorless. Creates a cleaner, more neutral mayonnaise that lets the olive oil and egg yolk flavors speak more clearly.

best_times.rating: ⭐ Ideal — serve with spring vegetables, salads, cold seafood
Very good — mayonnaise is a year-round condiment for any cold preparation

name: Garlic Mayonnaise (Alioli con huevo)
subtitle: The Mediterranean's most aromatic emulsion

enhancement.cookbook_narrative: True alioli — al i oli, 'garlic and oil' in Catalan — is one of the oldest sauces in the Western culinary canon, a pure liaison of raw garlic and olive oil made in a mortar that predates the egg entirely. The egg-enriched version Roden gives us here is the more recent, more stable, and more approachable form — what the rest of the world calls aioli. The garlic is its identity: not a background note but the entire point. Three to five cloves is not excessive. Used with the right foods — grilled seafood, rabbit, artichokes, boiled rice — alioli is not merely a condiment but the flavor architecture of the dish. The Catalan tradition adds a drop of honey; the Valencian version is softer; the Balearic version is made with only garlic and oil, the original way, and is so thick a knife could stand in it.

sources: salt to taste via garlic paste
salt.note: Crush garlic with a pinch of salt — the abrasion of salt crystals against garlic in a mortar breaks down the cells more effectively than crushing alone, releasing more allicin and creating a finer paste.

fat.status: ✓ Fat-dominant — the entire sauce is emulsified oil
fat.note: The fat ratio and selection from the Mayonesa recipe (0007) applies exactly here. Same technique, same oil blend.

acid.status: ⚠ No acid specified — add lemon juice for brightness and emulsion stability
warnings: No acid listed — consider adding 10–15ml lemon juice to brighten and stabilize
acid.note: Garlic alioli without acid can taste heavy and monolithic. A small squeeze of lemon juice at the finish brightens the garlic flavor and balances the fat.

heat.status: ✓ No heat — raw preparation
heat.note: All ingredients must be at room temperature, as with the base mayonnaise recipe.

science_notes.topic: Garlic Allicin — Raw Intensity vs. Cooked Sweetness
science_notes.body: Raw garlic contains alliin, which is converted to allicin when the cell walls are broken. Allicin is extremely pungent and sharp — the compound responsible for garlic breath and the burning sensation of raw garlic. When garlic is cooked, allicin transforms into milder, sweeter compounds including diallyl sulfides. In a raw alioli, the full allicin intensity is present and intentional.
science_notes.applied: Crush garlic to a smooth paste with a flat of a knife and a pinch of salt before incorporating. A coarser crush retains more allicin and produces sharper flavor; a smoother paste distributes it more evenly through the sauce.

science_notes.topic: The Traditional Mortar Method — Why It Makes a Different Product
science_notes.body: Traditional alioli made without egg in a mortar and pestle creates an unstable emulsion held together entirely by water from the garlic's own cells and the mucilage of its crushed fibers. This emulsion breaks easily but, when it works, produces an extraordinarily rich, intensely flavored paste unlike anything made in a food processor.
science_notes.applied: For the egg-free traditional version, crush 8–10 cloves of garlic to a smooth paste in a mortar, adding oil drop by drop and working the pestle in one direction only. The process takes 20 minutes and produces approximately 200ml of the most powerful garlic sauce in existence.

technique_notes.intro: The garlic paste is the heart of alioli. It must be smooth enough to incorporate into the emulsion evenly.

rows.value: 3 cloves for a mild alioli; 5 for the traditional assertive Catalan version.
Use the flat of a knife + salt to crush, then chop and smear repeatedly until a smooth paste forms.
Mortar and pestle creates a finer paste with more allicin released — for maximum garlic intensity.
Chunky, under-crushed garlic creates hot spots of intense flavor rather than even distribution throughout the sauce.

technique_notes.intro: The technique is identical to the base mayonnaise, with garlic paste incorporated into the egg yolk base before oil is added.

rows.value: Whisk garlic paste into the egg yolks before adding any oil. The garlic integrates more evenly this way.
Same technique as mayonnaise — drop by drop for the first 50ml, then a steady thin stream.
Add a squeeze of lemon juice at the end to brighten and balance the garlic's punch.
Same as mayonnaise — cold ingredients and too-fast oil addition are the primary causes of failure.

complete.label: Garlic + olive oil + lemon — Mediterranean classic ✦
complete.body: The trio of garlic, olive oil, and lemon is one of the most deeply rooted flavor affinities in Mediterranean cooking. Together they create a balance of pungent-fat-acid that the palate recognizes as fundamentally right — a combination that appears in hundreds of forms from skordalia to gremolata to this alioli.
complete.source: The Flavor Bible — garlic essential pairings

flavor_combinations.note: The Catalan tradition adds a single drop of honey to the finished alioli — not enough to taste sweet, but enough to round the garlic's sharpness. This tiny addition makes a significant difference in the overall balance of the sauce.
A pinch of saffron dissolved in the lemon juice before incorporation creates the golden all-i-oli amb safrà, traditional in some Valencian preparations for rice dishes and grilled fish.

enhancements.title: Serve alioli at room temperature for full garlic flavor impact
enhancements.body: Cold suppresses volatile aromatic compounds including allicin's aroma precursors. Alioli served straight from the refrigerator tastes flat and mild compared to the same sauce at room temperature. The garlic is there in both — but only the room-temperature version announces itself properly.
enhancements.how: Remove from the refrigerator at least 20 minutes before serving. If made ahead, store covered to prevent the surface from drying and developing a skin.
sources: On Food and Cooking — Harold McGee, allium volatile chemistry

enhancements.title: Add the tiniest drop of honey — a traditional Catalan technique to round the garlic
enhancements.body: The Catalan alioli tradition includes a single drop — not a teaspoon — of honey in the finished sauce. It does not make the alioli sweet; it rounds the aggressive allicin edge of the raw garlic and creates a more harmonious, balanced sauce. This is the kind of detail that separates a good alioli from a great one.
enhancements.how: Dip a teaspoon tip into honey and add to the finished alioli. Stir thoroughly. Taste — the honey should be imperceptible as such; it merely softens.
sources: The Food of Spain — Claudia Roden, variation note; The Flavor Bible — honey + garlic pairing

beverage_pairing.primary: Cold Cava brut or Albariño
beverage_pairing.why: The same pairing as the base mayonnaise, but garlic's intensity calls for slightly more acid and carbonation to cut through both the fat and the allicin sharpness.

tiers.label: +1 Great
tiers.items: Brut Cava, Albariño, Txakoli, cold dry Manzanilla

tiers.label: 0 Good
tiers.items: Sauvignon Blanc, Pinot Grigio, cold lager

tiers.label: 0 Neutral
tiers.items: sparkling water with lemon

tiers.label: Avoid
tiers.items: tannic reds — garlic sulfur compounds + tannin = metallic, harsh clash; oaked whites overwhelm the delicate egg emulsion

rule.label: Raw Garlic and Tannin Clash
rule.text: Raw garlic's sulfur compounds interact with wine tannins to create a metallic, bitter flavor. This is not a subtle difference — it is genuinely unpleasant. Always choose low-tannin, high-acid wines or cold beer with raw-garlic preparations.

beyond.rec: Estrella Damm or cold pilsner
beyond.why: Cold beer's carbonation cuts through the fat coating while its mild bitterness provides a pleasant contrast to the rich, garlicky sauce. The standard Catalan tapa bar companion.

beyond.rec: Cold green tea with a slice of lemon
beyond.why: Green tea's light tannins are gentle enough not to clash with the garlic, and the lemon bridges to the alioli's own acid component.

beyond.rec: Sparkling water with lemon and fresh mint
beyond.why: Carbonation + acid + cooling mint is the ideal palate cleanser between bites of garlic-rich alioli.

Raw garlic → intensity and character.name: Roasted garlic (double quantity)
Raw garlic → intensity and character.note: Roasted garlic is sweeter, nuttier, and far milder than raw. Double the quantity of roasted garlic to approximate the same flavor impact. Creates a completely different but delicious sauce.

Raw garlic → intensity and character.name: Black garlic (1–2 cloves)
Raw garlic → intensity and character.note: Fermented black garlic has a sweet, molasses-like depth with mild garlic flavor. Produces an unusually complex, dark-flavored alioli excellent with grilled meats.

ingredients.name: Garlic
ingredients.season: Year-round; fresh new season garlic June–August

best_times.rating: ⭐ Ideal — fresh garlic at peak intensity; grilled fish and vegetables need alioli
Very good — essential condiment across all seasons

name: Quince Alioli (Alioli de membrillo)
subtitle: Catalan fruit and garlic's unlikely harmony

enhancement.cookbook_narrative: This is one of the most unexpectedly beautiful things in the Spanish sauce repertoire — quince's honeyed, astringent perfume meeting garlic and good mayonnaise in a combination that should not work but does, spectacularly.
