partial.body: During Lent, this soup traditionally includes desalted bacalao (salt cod) added with the spinach in the final minutes. The bacalao adds a marine, saline depth that the vegetarian version lacks. A small 100g piece of desalted bacalao, flaked and stirred in at the end, transforms the soup.
partial.source: The Food of Spain — Claudia Roden, Lenten variation

flavor_combinations.note: The traditional Lenten version includes salt cod added in the final 5 minutes. The bacalao's briny, saline depth gives the vegetarian base a dimension of marine umami that dramatically enriches the soup. This is the canonical recipe for Semana Santa (Holy Week) in Castile.

The chopped hard-boiled egg Roden specifies is the traditional Lenten protein — in the absence of meat, eggs provided richness and satiation. Finely chopped and scattered over the finished soup, the egg yolk also enriches the broth slightly.

enhancements.title: Fry the picada bread to deep golden-amber — this is the single most important technique step
enhancements.body: The picada is this soup's genius, but only if the bread is properly fried. Pale fried bread produces starchy paste; deeply golden-amber fried bread produces a complex, Maillard-rich paste that enriches the entire soup. The colour target is the colour of a toasted chestnut — amber, not pale gold, not dark brown. This takes 3 minutes of careful attention and changes everything.
enhancements.how: Heat olive oil in a small pan until it shimmers. Add bread slices and fry until amber on both sides — 90 seconds per side. Do not lower the heat. The bread should be golden-brown, fragrant, and slightly crisp.
sources: Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat — Samin Nosrat, Maillard reaction; The Flavor Bible — fried bread + garlic + spice

enhancements.title: Add wine vinegar off-heat after the picada has dissolved — the acid finish is structurally essential
enhancements.body: Chickpeas and potato are two of the starchiest, most acid-resistant ingredients in Spanish cooking. Without vinegar, this potaje can taste dense, heavy, and one-dimensional despite all the picada's work. The 15ml of wine vinegar added off-heat and tasted immediately provides the essential brightness that lifts all the other flavours.
enhancements.how: After the picada has dissolved and the spinach has wilted, remove from heat. Add 10–15ml of red wine vinegar, stir well, and taste. Add a little more if needed. The soup should taste earthy AND bright simultaneously.
sources: Salt, Fat, Acid, Heat — Samin Nosrat, acid as a finishing structural element

beverage_pairing.primary: Fino sherry or dry Manzanilla
beverage_pairing.why: The Moorish spice profile of this soup (cumin, pimentón, garlic) calls for a wine with enough minerality and acid to stand against it without competing. Fino sherry's lean, saline austerity is the perfect counterpoint to the soup's earthy richness.

tiers.label: +2 Ethereal
tiers.items: Fino sherry, dry Manzanilla, dry Amontillado

tiers.label: +1 Great
tiers.items: Albariño, dry Verdejo, cold pilsner

tiers.label: 0 Good
tiers.items: light lager, sparkling water with lemon

tiers.label: Avoid
tiers.items: tannic reds — cumin + pimentón + tannin creates a harsh, bitter combination; sweet wines undermine the vinegar finish

rule.label: Spice Needs Acid and Minerality, Not Tannin
rule.text: Cumin and pimentón are warm, earthy spices that are amplified by tannin into bitterness. Choose wines with high acid and low tannin alongside spiced dishes — the acid complements while the absence of tannin prevents the harsh clash.

beyond.rec: Cold Spanish or Moroccan lager (Casablanca, Mahou)
beyond.why: A cold, light lager is the perfect pairing for a spiced Moorish-influenced soup — refreshing, neutral, and an ideal palate cleanser between bowls.

beyond.rec: Moroccan mint tea (hot, sweetened)
beyond.why: The cumin and pimentón in this soup trace directly to Moorish culinary traditions shared with Morocco. Moroccan mint tea — hot, fragrant, sweet — is a historically resonant pairing that acknowledges the soup's Andalusian-Moorish origins.

beyond.rec: Sparkling water with lemon and a pinch of cumin and salt
beyond.why: A Moroccan-inspired spiced sparkling water mirrors the soup's own spice profile in liquid form — aromatic, acid, and refreshing.

Tinned chickpeas → legume.name: Dried chickpeas (soaked 12 hours, cooked)
Tinned chickpeas → legume.note: Produce a considerably better-tasting potaje — more tender, more flavourful, more cohesive texture. The 12-hour soak and 1-hour cooking time is the only obstacle.

Tinned chickpeas → legume.name: White cannellini beans
Tinned chickpeas → legume.note: Create a creamier, softer potaje. Good substitution but loses the chickpea's characteristic firm-tender bite.

Fresh spinach → green element.name: Frozen spinach (defrosted, squeezed dry)
Fresh spinach → green element.note: Convenient and works well. Squeeze firmly to remove excess water or the broth will be diluted. Equal weight to fresh.

Fresh spinach → green element.name: Swiss chard (stems removed, leaves only)
Fresh spinach → green element.note: Adds a slightly more mineral, earthy quality than spinach. Beautiful in the potaje and nutritionally richer.

best_times.rating: ⭐ Ideal — traditional season; the soup's cultural meaning is fully present
⭐ Ideal — warming, deeply satisfying, spinach at peak
Very good — good transition food; fresh spinach excellent
Good — lighter on a warm day if served with less picada
